picks.name: NVIDIA Corporation
picks.rationale: NVDA's data center revenue hit $62.3B in Q4 FY2026 (+75% YoY) with next-quarter guidance of ~$78B, making it the irreplaceable backbone of the $754B hyperscale AI capex cycle. The thesis remains intact: every major cloud provider is capacity-constrained on H100/B200 allocations, and Blackwell demand has not peaked. The macro briefing confirms AI-infrastructure enablers are expected to account for roughly half of S&P 500 earnings growth in 2026, and Tepper's Appaloosa — despite trimming — still holds NVDA as a core position alongside massive adds in MU and SNDK (the memory complement), confirming institutional conviction in the AI silicon stack.
picks.catalyst: Q2 FY2027 earnings (late August 2026) with Blackwell ramp cadence and China re-export license update
picks.entryNote: Extended at ~35x forward sales with RSI historically piercing 75–80; best entry on pullbacks toward the 50dma. Valuation premium is earned by the unmatched earnings revision trajectory but late-stage rally risk is elevated — size accordingly and do not chase gaps.
picks.exitTrigger: Hyperscaler capex guidance cut of 20%+ for 2027, or evidence of NVDA custom silicon displacement at a second major cloud customer beyond Google TPU
picks.keyRisk: AI capex confidence collapse — a single hyperscaler announcing ROI-driven CapEx deferral could trigger a 30–50% de-rating

picks.name: Meta Platforms, Inc.
picks.rationale: META is the highest-quality compounder in the Magnificent 7 by factor combination — operating margins have expanded to ~45%, ROIC is rising, and FCF yield (~3.5%) is exceptional for a hyper-growth name. Crucially, META is a capex *beneficiary* not just a spender: its custom MTIA silicon deal with Broadcom confirms disciplined AI infrastructure deployment that improves ad-targeting ROI rather than burning cash on unmonetized GPU racks. FY2026 EPS estimates have been revised upward 5+ consecutive weeks with 4/4 trailing EPS beats, and the momentum screen ranks it #4 overall with no 'extended' flag.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — advertising revenue inflection from Llama-powered Advantage+ targeting and Reality Labs margin trajectory
picks.entryNote: Not flagged as extended; momentum is fundamentals-led rather than multiple-expansion driven. Forward P/E ~23x is reasonable given 40%+ operating margins and double-digit revenue growth.
picks.exitTrigger: Operating margin compression below 38% for two consecutive quarters, or evidence of advertiser budget rotation away from Meta's platforms toward competing AI-native channels
picks.keyRisk: Regulatory antitrust action (EU DMA enforcement or U.S. FTC breakup proceedings) that forces divestiture of Instagram or WhatsApp

picks.name: Alphabet Inc.
picks.rationale: Alphabet screens as a high-conviction carry-over: Berkshire under Greg Abel nearly tripled its GOOGL stake to ~57.8M shares (~$16.6B), making it their 5th-largest position — an unusually strong smart-money endorsement from a historically conservative allocator. Post the –10% scare on AI capex concerns, GOOGL has stabilized; Google Cloud is growing 30%+ YoY and Search advertising remains structurally dominant even as Perplexity/ChatGPT erode at the margins. At ~20x forward earnings, GOOGL is the cheapest Magnificent 7 compounder with AI optionality on Cloud, Waymo autonomous miles, and DeepMind drug discovery.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — Google Cloud revenue acceleration and AI Overviews ad monetization update
picks.entryNote: GOOGL trading well off its recent highs following the AI-capex selloff; ~20x forward EPS represents a meaningful discount to its 5-year average of ~25x and is a compelling entry relative to the quality of the franchise.
picks.exitTrigger: DOJ-ordered search divestiture or Google Cloud growth decelerating below 20% YoY for two consecutive quarters
picks.keyRisk: DOJ antitrust remedy mandating structural separation of Google Search from Chrome/Android distribution

picks.name: Goldman Sachs Group, Inc.
picks.rationale: GS equity traders are tracking to produce more than $5B revenue in Q2 2026 — another blockbuster quarter — and Citi raised its price target to $1,100, with the stock trading at ~$1,090 today. The macro regime is ideal for Goldman: a bear-steepening curve, re-accelerating M&A/IPO pipelines (SpaceX's $89B bond demand and Lime's $1.8B NYC IPO underscore deal flow reacceleration), and IG OAS near multi-year tights enabling primary market issuance. The financial conditions regime (steep curve + resilient credit) directly expands Goldman's trading and advisory revenue, and at ~1.2x book with rising ROIC the valuation is not stretched.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (mid-July) — equity trading revenue and IB advisory fee backlog update
picks.entryNote: GS at ~$1,090 is off its 52-week high of $1,125 but well above its 52-week low of $653; Wells Fargo reiterated Buy today. At ~12x forward earnings, the stock trades at a modest premium to tangible book — reasonable given the ROIC trajectory.
picks.exitTrigger: HY OAS widening above 500bps (credit stress) or sustained yield curve inversion returning to –50bps on 2s10s, which would compress NIMs and deal flow simultaneously
picks.keyRisk: Sudden credit market dislocation or recession that freezes capital markets activity and forces loan-loss provisioning across the banking sector

picks.name: S&P Global Inc.
picks.rationale: SPGI is a compounding machine in exactly the right macro environment: re-accelerating issuance (IG OAS near multi-year tights = record corporate bond issuance fees for Ratings), AI-driven data product monetization (Market Intelligence and Ratings benefit from AI-driven features), and the pending Mobility spin-off approved in May 2026 which will unlock substantial value and sharpen the core franchise focus. Trading at ~$400 vs. 24-analyst consensus target of $533.76 (+31% upside) with a 'Strong Buy' consensus, SPGI is the cleanest way to own a toll-road on global capital markets activity without taking direct credit or rate risk.
picks.catalyst: Mobility division spin-off completion (H2 2026) and Q2 2026 earnings showing Ratings revenue acceleration from record issuance volumes
picks.entryNote: SPGI at ~$400 is near its 52-week low of $381.61, down sharply from its high of $579.05 — a rare opportunity to buy this quality franchise at a 30%+ discount to analyst consensus fair value. P/E of ~25x is at the low end of its historical range for a business with structural pricing power.
picks.exitTrigger: Corporate bond issuance volumes collapsing >40% YoY (recession/credit freeze) or antitrust-forced divestiture of the Ratings business
picks.keyRisk: Regulatory action breaking up the SPGI-MCO ratings duopoly, which remains an ongoing EU and DOJ area of interest

picks.name: Broadcom Inc.
picks.rationale: Broadcom is the highest-quality 'picks and shovels' AI semiconductor name: AI revenue jumped 106% YoY in fiscal Q1 2026 to $8.4B (43% of total revenue), and the customer lock-in moat is extraordinary — multi-year TPU contracts with Alphabet through 2031, MTIA accelerators with Meta, and OpenAI as its sixth custom silicon customer. Druckenmiller's Duquesne initiated a new AVGO position in Q1 2026, rotating out of Alphabet into AI hardware infrastructure. The –13% post-earnings reaction on margin optics created an accumulation entry that the fundamental thesis does not justify.
picks.catalyst: Q3 FY2026 earnings (September) — AI revenue as a share of total and VMware integration margin trajectory
picks.entryNote: Post-earnings dip to accumulation levels; the -13% reaction on optics rather than fundamentals is the entry thesis. Forward P/E ~28x is elevated vs. history but reasonable given 40%+ AI segment growth and customer concentration lock-in.
picks.exitTrigger: Loss of a hyperscaler custom silicon design win (especially Google TPU or Meta MTIA contract cancellation), or VMware integration costs materially exceeding guidance for two consecutive quarters
picks.keyRisk: Custom silicon substitution risk — if hyperscalers develop in-house alternatives to Broadcom's networking ASICs at scale

picks.name: RTX Corporation
picks.rationale: RTX is the premier quality compounder within defense aerospace — combining Pratt & Whitney's irreplaceable engine aftermarket franchise (decades-long service revenue streams on GTF engines) with Raytheon's missile/air defense business seeing structural demand acceleration from NATO rearming and Middle East tensions. The sector rotation scorecard confirms Industrials are 'supported by increased capital spending in key growth areas like electricity capacity, construction around the AI infrastructure buildout, defense, and energy.' Defense budgets are structurally rising across NATO allies following Russia-Ukraine and Middle East conflicts, creating a multi-year backlog runway that is largely immune to the Fed's hawkish hold.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — Pratt & Whitney GTF engine shop visit revenue acceleration and Raytheon missile order backlog update
picks.entryNote: RTX has been a consistent portfolio anchor; trading at ~19x forward EPS vs. a historical average of ~17x — a modest premium justified by the accelerating defense cycle and aftermarket compounding. Relative strength vs. SPY has been consistently positive.
picks.exitTrigger: Pratt & Whitney GTF powder metal disk remediation costs exceeding $10B total (material balance sheet impairment) or a sudden U.S. defense budget sequestration of >10%
picks.keyRisk: GTF engine remediation scope expansion that materially impairs cash generation and forces a dividend cut or equity raise

picks.name: Micron Technology, Inc.
picks.rationale: Micron's record Q3 fiscal 2026 print is a bellwether for memory demand and hyperscaler capex — HBM3E supply remains severely constrained with every unit sold into AI accelerator builds at premium pricing, driving DRAM ASP expansion not seen since 2021. Tepper's Appaloosa added meaningfully to MU in Q1 2026 alongside a new SanDisk position, betting on the AI memory supercycle. The macro briefing notes 'chip producers are extending their historic rally, with Micron and SanDisk adding 5% on the day' and today's StockTitan confirms MU's record Q3 as a market bellwether — the earnings revision cycle has only just begun as HBM3E shipments accelerate.
picks.catalyst: Q4 FY2026 earnings guidance (late September) — HBM3E shipment volumes and DRAM/NAND pricing trajectory into 2027
picks.entryNote: MU has pulled back following the Asia chip sell-off flagged in today's macro update, creating a re-entry opportunity. Trades at ~9x forward EV/EBITDA — cheap for a memory cycle peak, but HBM3E dynamics have structurally changed the margin profile vs. prior cycles.
picks.exitTrigger: HBM3E market share loss to SK Hynix exceeding 60%+ of total HBM shipments, or AI capex guidance cuts from two or more major hyperscalers simultaneously
picks.keyRisk: Memory cycle downturn — if AI training demand plateaus, DRAM/NAND pricing reverts and MU's margins compress rapidly as a commodity supplier

picks.name: Exxon Mobil Corporation
picks.rationale: Energy has overtaken Industrials as the #1 sector on relative strength across all timeframes, with XOM as one of the two biggest contributors (+22% YTD for the sector). The macro regime is structurally constructive: PPI at 6.0% YoY signals persistent input cost inflation that benefits commodity producers, and while oil has pulled back today as Iran peace talks progress, the structural supply constraint (years of underinvestment in upstream, AI data center power demand) keeps energy in a different equilibrium than prior cycles. At ~12x forward earnings vs. a 13x historical average, XOM is the rare case of a sector leader trading below its own history with a 3%+ dividend yield as a return floor.
picks.catalyst: Iran nuclear deal resolution (within 2 months per macro briefing) — paradoxically, a ceasefire scenario would test whether XOM's LNG/Permian free cash flow can sustain buybacks even at $70 oil
picks.entryNote: Today's oil pullback to pre-war levels provides a better entry vs. last week's elevated geopolitical risk premium. XOM at fair-to-cheap valuation with a best-in-class balance sheet (AA- rated) and $35B+ annual buyback program provides asymmetric downside protection.
picks.exitTrigger: Brent crude sustained below $65/barrel for 90+ days (Iran deal + demand destruction) combined with management reducing buyback guidance, signaling balance sheet stress
picks.keyRisk: Iran deal fully resolving with 2–3 Mbpd of supply restoration driving a rapid oil price normalization to $65–$70, compressing upstream margins for 12–18 months

picks.name: Quanta Services, Inc.
picks.rationale: Quanta is the best pure-play on the electricity infrastructure supercycle that sits at the intersection of AI data center power demand, grid modernization, and renewable energy interconnection — all three secular tailwinds are compounding simultaneously. The sector scorecard notes Industrials are 'supported by increased capital spending in key growth areas like electricity capacity, construction around the AI infrastructure buildout, defense, and energy' — PWR is the essential contractor executing that buildout. Unlike VST (flagged with extended/leverage risk), PWR is an asset-light services model with high backlog visibility, strong FCF conversion, and multi-year contracted revenue that provides earnings predictability in a volatile macro environment.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (August) — electric power backlog update and data center hyperscaler contract wins disclosure
picks.entryNote: PWR has been a consistent portfolio presence; the electrification/AI infrastructure buildout has years of runway and any infrastructure spending bill tailwind is additive. Forward P/E ~25x represents fair value for a business growing revenue at 15%+ with high backlog visibility.
picks.exitTrigger: Hyperscaler data center buildout deferrals reducing grid interconnection demand, or labor cost inflation compressing EBITDA margins below 9% for two consecutive quarters
picks.keyRisk: Execution risk on rapid workforce and project scaling — labor shortages in skilled electrical trades could compress margins and delay deliveries on multi-billion dollar contracts

picks.name: SPDR Gold Shares
picks.rationale: Gold is the definitive macro hedge for the current regime: stagflationary inflation (May CPI 4.2%, PPI 6.0%), fiscal deficit running 7–7.5% of GDP with debt projected to exceed 140% of GDP by 2031, Fed credibility risk under new Chair Warsh, and foreign central banks now holding more gold than U.S. Treasuries. Goldman lowered its 2026 gold target to $4,900 from $5,400 citing potential rate hikes — which paradoxically confirms the regime where gold hedges are most needed. Having pulled back ~20% from its January 2026 peak, GLD offers a disciplined re-entry with asymmetric upside in both the inflation-persistence and fiscal-stress scenarios.
picks.catalyst: Thursday May PCE release (June 25) — a hot print above 3.7% reinforces stagflation thesis and directly benefits GLD
picks.entryNote: GLD ~20% off January 2026 ATH; re-entry at technically constructive levels with the next PCE print as an immediate catalyst. The pullback from ~$495 to ~$397 resets the risk/reward favorably given structural demand from central banks and the de-dollarization trend.
picks.exitTrigger: Fed pivot to aggressive easing (2+ cuts in 90 days) combined with Iran deal fully resolving and oil falling below $65 — a genuine disinflationary scenario that removes the core gold thesis
picks.keyRisk: Rapid real yield normalization (10-year TIPS yield rising above 2.5%) driven by a Fed surprise hike that also tightens financial conditions materially

picks.name: Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF
picks.rationale: SCHD provides broad dividend-quality exposure — financials, industrials, consumer staples, and healthcare — which is the precise basket benefiting from the 'Old Economy taking the baton' rotation confirmed in the sector scorecard. With a 3.5%+ dividend yield and a portfolio screened for consistent dividend growth and financial health, SCHD is both an income anchor and a quality factor tilt. In a late-cycle, sticky-inflation environment, dividend growers compound total return even if price appreciation moderates, providing a return floor the 10-year Treasury cannot match on a risk-adjusted basis.
picks.catalyst: Continued broadening of market breadth away from mega-cap tech into dividend-paying sectors; small-cap Russell 2000 leading YTD confirms the rotation is durable
picks.entryNote: SCHD has been a consistent defensive sleeve anchor; current entry is supported by the confirmed rotation toward dividend-quality names. The ETF's sector mix naturally underweights the high-multiple tech names most vulnerable to rate-driven multiple compression.
picks.exitTrigger: If the macro regime shifts to outright recession with HY spreads above 700bps, dividend sustainability in cyclical sectors (Financials, Industrials) would be impaired — reduce to VYM or IEF at that point
picks.keyRisk: Dividend cuts in the financial sector if a credit event materializes and banks are forced to preserve capital, disproportionately impacting SCHD's largest weights

picks.name: Berkshire Hathaway Inc. Class B
picks.rationale: Berkshire is the ultimate 'sleep at night' holding: $397.4B in cash/T-bills (the largest cash hoard in its history), a diversified insurance/energy/rail/consumer franchise that generates cash through any cycle, and Greg Abel actively repositioning the equity book toward quality compounders (GOOGL +225%, new DAL). At ~1.4x book value, BRK.B sits near the historical floor where Buffett himself has repurchased shares. With a beta under 0.7 and zero AI narrative premium to unwind, BRK.B is the portfolio's shock absorber — in a 30–40% growth-multiple compression scenario, Berkshire's fortress balance sheet becomes a relative safe haven and a dry powder deployment vehicle.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 13F filing (August 14) — further Abel-era portfolio repositioning may reveal new positions that catalyze re-rating; stress test deployment of cash hoard into dislocated assets
picks.entryNote: BRK.B near 1.4x book is at the lower end of its post-pandemic valuation range; the $397B cash pile earns ~$20B/year in T-bill income alone, providing a significant earnings floor that is increasingly underappreciated by the market.
picks.exitTrigger: Deployment of cash hoard into a large acquisition at a materially expensive multiple (>2.5x book for an insurance target) signaling capital allocation discipline erosion
picks.keyRisk: CEO transition uncertainty — Greg Abel has not yet faced a major economic downturn as decision-maker, and his capital allocation discipline remains unproven at the scale of a genuine crisis

picks.name: iShares 7-10 Year Treasury Bond ETF
picks.rationale: IEF offers ~4.3% yield with moderate 7.5-year duration — the sweet spot between income generation and convexity. With the 10-year at 4.49% and today's briefing noting it has slipped back below 4.50% on oil falling to pre-war levels, IEF is a tactical tail hedge against AI capex collapse or sudden growth deceleration. In a genuine risk-off scenario, the 7–10 year Treasury sector rallies sharply while avoiding the fiscal-risk concentration of TLT (18+ year duration). The PCE print on June 25 is the near-term binary: a cool print strengthens IEF; a hot print is the known risk, which is already reflected in current yields.
picks.catalyst: May PCE release (June 25) — a benign print near/below 3.5% would trigger a Treasury rally and IEF appreciation; also serves as tail hedge if equities sell off on a hot print
picks.entryNote: 10-year yield at 4.49% is at the high end of its recent range and near the level where the Fed's own models suggest restrictive policy; IEF at current levels locks in 4.3% yield with meaningful upside if the macro surprises to the downside.
picks.exitTrigger: 10-year Treasury yield rising sustainably above 5.25% (fiscal stress / bond vigilante scenario) indicating IEF duration risk outweighs the defensive benefit — rotate to shorter T-bills
picks.keyRisk: Bond vigilante episode driven by fiscal deficit concerns forcing the 10-year above 5.25–5.50%, inflicting mark-to-market losses that overwhelm the coupon income

picks.name: The Coca-Cola Company
picks.rationale: KO completes the defensive sleeve's consumer staples exposure as a lower-beta, dividend-compounding anchor that has historically outperformed in late-cycle and stagflationary environments. Unlike Walmart and Costco (rated 1-star 'significantly overvalued' by Morningstar), KO is a reasonably valued global compounder with pricing power that has demonstrated it can pass through inflation to consumers, generating mid-single-digit organic revenue growth. With a 3%+ dividend yield, Berkshire as its largest institutional shareholder providing a structural ownership floor, and a business model immune to AI disruption or rate sensitivity, KO is the portfolio's lowest-volatility position.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — organic revenue growth confirmation and pricing realization in emerging markets amid USD stabilization
picks.entryNote: KO has been periodically included in this portfolio as a defensive anchor; at ~22x forward earnings it is fairly valued for a business with near-monopoly brand economics and consistent dividend growth for 60+ consecutive years.
picks.exitTrigger: Sustained volume decline of >5% globally for two consecutive quarters indicating demand elasticity breaking down as pricing power peaks, or a GLP-1/sugar-tax regulatory wave materially compressing carbonated beverage volumes
picks.keyRisk: GLP-1 drug adoption reducing caloric beverage consumption at scale, a structural demand headwind that has not yet been priced into consensus estimates

summary: The portfolio is positioned for a late-cycle stagflationary inflection — Fed on hold at 3.50–3.75% with hawkish dot plot, May CPI at 4.2%, PPI at 6.0%, and term-premium decompression driving bear-steepening — where the equity risk premium is compressed but earnings revisions are still broadly positive thanks to the AI capex supercycle. The 10 growth picks express three complementary themes: AI infrastructure compounders (NVDA, AVGO, MU) that benefit from the $754B hyperscale capex cycle; high-quality platform businesses with durable competitive moats and rising FCF (META, GOOGL, GS, SPGI); and real-economy electrification/energy plays (RTX, PWR, XOM) that hedge against stagflation and provide sector diversification. Carry-overs from prior days are NVDA, META, GOOGL, GS, SPGI, RTX, PWR, XOM, and MU — all of which retain intact theses confirmed by today's data (GS Q2 equity revenue tracking >$5B, MU record Q3 print confirmed, SPGI at 31% analyst upside); AAPL is dropped in favor of SPGI given SPGI's more compelling valuation dislocation (~30% below consensus target) and the Mobility spin-off catalyst. The defensive sleeve (GLD, SCHD, BRK.B, IEF, KO) spans gold/real assets, dividend equity, cash-fortress equity, intermediate duration, and consumer staples — providing genuine diversification of defensive mechanisms. The dominant risk being underwritten is an AI capex confidence collapse: if two or more hyperscalers cut 2027 CapEx guidance materially, NVDA, AVGO, and MU would face simultaneous de-rating, representing ~25% of the growth book weight — the defensive sleeve and XOM/RTX/PWR diversification are sized precisely to limit total portfolio drawdown in that scenario.
diversificationNote: The 10 growth picks span 5 distinct GICS sectors: Information Technology (NVDA, AVGO, MU — 3 names, at the hard cap), Communication Services (META, GOOGL), Financials (GS, SPGI), Industrials (RTX, PWR), and Energy (XOM); within IT, NVDA (GPUs/compute), AVGO (custom ASICs/networking), and MU (memory/HBM3E) address different points in the AI value chain, reducing correlation despite sharing a secular demand driver. The defensive sleeve spans four distinct exposures: gold/real assets (GLD), broad dividend-quality equity (SCHD), cash-fortress equity (BRK.B), intermediate-duration Treasuries (IEF), and consumer staples (KO) — no exposure is doubled up.

picks.name: NVIDIA Corporation
picks.rationale: NVDA remains the irreplaceable toll road of the AI capex supercycle — Vera Rubin server ramp, Blackwell Ultra demand, and sovereign AI deployments globally underpin the earnings revision cycle. At ~$210 today, the stock has consolidated from its highs and Nvidia just completed a $25B multi-tranche debt offering signaling management confidence in long-term infrastructure investment. With AI-infrastructure beneficiaries expected to account for roughly half of all S&P 500 earnings growth in 2026, NVDA is the highest-conviction single name. Tiger Global holds it as a core position.
picks.catalyst: Q2 FY2027 earnings report (est. late August) with Vera Rubin server ramp update and Blackwell Ultra shipment guidance
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~$210, consolidating well off recent highs; ~46% analyst-consensus upside per TipRanks screen; accumulating above $200 50dma support is the disciplined entry zone
picks.exitTrigger: Hyperscaler capex guidance cuts from two or more of MSFT/AMZN/META/GOOGL in a single quarter, or data center revenue growth decelerates below 100% YoY
picks.keyRisk: AI capex monetization collapse — hyperscalers pause buildout if AI ROI fails to materialize, triggering synchronized de-rating across the AI supply chain

picks.name: Alphabet Inc.
picks.rationale: Berkshire added 224% to its Alphabet position in Q1 2026 — pushing GOOGL to the #7 holding at ~$16.6B — the single most striking smart-money signal of the season, with Greg Abel making it one of his first bold bets. At ~$371, Google Cloud's 28%+ growth and Search AI Overviews monetization provide dual-engine earnings revision upside. World-class ~58% gross margins, improving ROIC trajectory, and best risk-adjusted profile in Mag-7 make this a core carry-over. Antitrust (AdX divestiture ruling) is the key overhang — sized at 10% to reflect that risk.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) with Google Cloud revenue trajectory and AI Overviews monetization update — plus DoJ adtech remedies ruling expected 2H 2026
picks.entryNote: At ~$371, trades at ~22x fwd EPS — reasonable given 15-20% EPS growth trajectory; well below MSFT/META multiples on comparable cloud/AI growth; consolidating above 50dma
picks.exitTrigger: Federal court mandates AdX forced divestiture AND Google Cloud growth decelerates below 20% YoY in the same quarter
picks.keyRisk: Forced structural divestiture of AdX search advertising business, which would compress earnings power and set antitrust cascade precedent for other Mag-7 names

picks.name: Amazon.com Inc.
picks.rationale: Tepper nearly doubled his Amazon stake and made it his top holding; Ackman moved it to #2 at Pershing Square; Tiger Global holds it as a core position — the highest concentration of smart-money validation in the universe. AWS is now growing 28-29% YoY driving operating margin expansion, and Amazon is reportedly exploring selling custom AI chips to other companies, expanding its TAM beyond AWS. At ~$246, the margin re-rating thesis from retail leverage and AWS operating income is the fundamental driver; FY26 EPS estimates revised higher across brokerages.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July/early August) — AWS revenue growth rate and first disclosure of Trainium chip external sales economics
picks.entryNote: At ~$246, below its 52-week high of $278.56; consolidating after a 25%+ recovery from the February $196 low; entering from a higher base is preferable — wait for pullbacks toward $235-240 if possible
picks.exitTrigger: AWS revenue growth decelerates below 20% YoY or retail operating margins revert below 4% as AI capex burns FCF
picks.keyRisk: FCF turns deeply negative in 2026 if hyperscaler AI capex accelerates faster than AWS revenue monetization, compressing equity valuation

picks.name: Meta Platforms Inc.
picks.rationale: META carries the strongest margin profile among Big Tech, with EPS of $7.14 vs. $5.75 consensus — a massive beat — and HSBC raised its price target to $900. AI-powered Advantage+ ad system is generating measurable ROI for advertisers, and Llama 4 monetization via API/enterprise is the next leg of the thesis. At ~$600, institutional accumulation is strong ahead of Llama monetization catalysts. This is the best-valued AI application layer name with a 35%+ operating margin and no regulatory binary imminent vs. GOOGL.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — Advantage+ revenue contribution disclosure and Llama 4 enterprise API pricing announcement
picks.entryNote: Trading ~$600; HSBC $900 PT implies 50%+ upside; forward P/E roughly 25x on strong EPS growth trajectory — justified given margin profile and AI monetization optionality; near 50dma support
picks.exitTrigger: Ad revenue growth decelerates below 15% YoY or Llama 4 fails to attract material enterprise contracts, suggesting AI investment is not monetizing
picks.keyRisk: Regulatory/antitrust cascade triggered by Alphabet AdX ruling extending to Meta's ad-market dominance, compressing ad pricing power

picks.name: The Goldman Sachs Group Inc.
picks.rationale: Goldman's Q1 2026 investment banking revenue surged 48% — the clearest proof that M&A and capital markets are thawing after years of suppression. At a forward P/E of 15.24x, GS trades at a 27% discount to its sector average. The bear steepener yield curve directly expands NIM for wholesale banks, and deregulatory tailwinds from the 'One Big Beautiful Bill' support buybacks and capital return. As the preeminent capital markets franchise globally, GS is the highest-beta beneficiary of the M&A/IPO reactivation cycle. Persistent carry-over from the prior week given intact thesis.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (mid-July) — investment banking revenue and trading revenue line items vs. elevated consensus expectations
picks.entryNote: Forward P/E 15.24x at 27% discount to sector; near 52-week high but fundamentals justify it — NIM expansion and capital markets re-rating are secular, not cyclical; trading above all key moving averages
picks.exitTrigger: Investment banking revenue reverts to flat YoY growth for two consecutive quarters or credit spreads widen above 150 bps signaling credit stress
picks.keyRisk: Sharp reversal in capital markets activity — geopolitical escalation or Fed hike execution could freeze M&A pipelines and compress trading revenues simultaneously

picks.name: S&P Global Inc.
picks.rationale: SPGI is a high-quality compounder benefiting from three simultaneous tailwinds: (1) bond issuance volume surge as corporate debt needs refinancing in a structurally higher-rate world, boosting Ratings revenue; (2) the approved spin-off of the Mobility division in May 2026 which incrementally unlocks value for shareholders; and (3) AI-driven demand for Market Intelligence data products and Benchmarks. RBC reiterated Buy with a $495 price target in May 2026. Near-monopoly ratings moat, 85%+ subscription revenues, and expanding FCF yield make this a pristine quality carry-over.
picks.catalyst: Mobility Division spin-off completion (H2 2026) and Q2 earnings (late July) — Ratings issuance volume in a high-yield and IG market both running hot
picks.entryNote: Buy rating reiterated with $495 PT; subscription-model revenue base provides earnings floor; valuation near mid-cycle average on a sum-of-parts basis post-Mobility separation
picks.exitTrigger: Primary bond issuance dries up (IG + HY monthly volumes fall >40% YoY for two consecutive months) or Mobility spin-off delayed beyond Q1 2027
picks.keyRisk: Regulatory scrutiny of ratings duopoly (SPGI + MCO) intensifying, or a sharp credit market freeze that halts bond issuance volumes

picks.name: RTX Corporation
picks.rationale: RTX is a dual-engine compounder with record backlog in both Raytheon defense (benefiting from structurally elevated NATO spending and Middle East tensions) and Collins Aerospace commercial aftermarket. Strong demand in both commercial and defense sectors is driving record backlog and robust sales, with long-term defense agreements and aftermarket strength underpin a positive multi-year outlook. A newly expanded landing gear facility in Poland signals NATO-aligned reshoring capex beneficiary. Defense spending is structurally elevated for 3-5+ years. This is a clean, low-risk industrial exposure with pricing power.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — Raytheon missile systems backlog update and Collins Aerospace aftermarket revenue momentum
picks.entryNote: Earnings slides posted May 29, 2026 confirming strong fundamentals; trading at ~20x fwd EPS — reasonable for a quality industrial with secular defense tailwind; above all key moving averages
picks.exitTrigger: A comprehensive US-Iran peace deal or NATO defense spending commitments drop below 2% GDP on average, reducing the structural demand for Raytheon missiles and Collins avionics

phaseData.macro:
---

# US Equity Macro Regime Briefing — Friday, June 19, 2026

---

## 📊 Data Dashboard

**Fed Policy**

The Federal Reserve held the federal funds rate at **3.50%–3.75%** at its June 17, 2026 meeting.
 
The updated dot plot was the real shock: nine of eighteen officials now expect at least one rate hike by year-end — a stark reversal from March's cut-leaning stance — and rate-hike odds nearly doubled overnight to **84%**.
 
The June SEP revised median PCE inflation to **3.6%** (from 2.7% in March) and core PCE to **3.3%** (from 2.7%), both well above the 2% target.
 The neutral rate (r*) is estimated near 2.5–3.0%, putting the **real policy rate at approximately +0.5% to +1.0%** — nominally restrictive, but less so given the inflation overshoot. 
Markets have effectively priced out any 2026 cut, with Nuveen now expecting the first move — still a cut — in H1 2027.


**Yield Curve**

As of June 17, the curve is upward-sloping: 2Y at **4.20%**, 5Y at **4.27%**, 10Y at **4.49%**, 30Y at **4.93%**. The 2s10s spread is **+29 bps** and the 3m10y spread is **+73 bps**.
 
Elevated term premium and steady Treasury issuance continue to buoy longer maturities.
 After years of inversion, the curve has re-normalized in a **bear steepener** fashion — not a bull steepener — which is the uncomfortable flavor driven by long-end supply and inflation risk premia, not by Fed easing.

**Inflation**

The separately reported May CPI ran hot at **4.2% year-over-year**.
 
Officials projected PCE price pressures to prove more persistent than previously expected, with headline PCE seen ending 2026 at **3.6%** and core PCE at **3.3%**.
 
Oil futures markets are pricing roughly **$85/barrel** through year-end, and tech-related mismeasurement continues to inflate core CPI readings
 — both factors keeping "supercore" (services ex-housing) re-accelerating rather than cooling. Trajectory: **re-accelerating / sticky**, not decelerating.

**Growth**

Per the Atlanta Fed's GDPNow model, real GDP growth is currently tracking at approximately **3% QoQ annualized** for Q2.
 
Payrolls have averaged approximately **190,000 per month** over the past three months, signaling a firmer labor market.
 
ISM Manufacturing PMI registered **52.7**, anchored by an increase in new orders as businesses rushed to avoid shortages from geopolitical disruption.
 
The Fed trimmed projected 2026 growth to **2.2%** and nudged its unemployment forecast to **4.3%**.


**Credit & Liquidity**

ICE BofA US HY OAS was **2.78%** (278 bps) in June 2026.
 
Broad IG OAS is running around **~80 bps**, with tighter spreads for AA issuers and wider for BBB.
 Both are well inside recessionary levels, suggesting credit is still in a risk-on posture even as equities wobbled post-FOMC. 
The DXY firmed near **100.80** following the hawkish FOMC, a level not seen since May 2025, after the Fed removed its reference to "additional rate adjustments."


**Earnings Backdrop**

Goldman Sachs raised its S&P 500 year-end 2026 forecast to **8,000** and lifted its 2026 EPS estimate to **$340** (representing ~24% annual growth).
 
AI-infrastructure beneficiaries are expected to account for roughly **half of all S&P 500 earnings growth** this year.
 
The valuation multiple is expected to hold flat at roughly **~21x forward earnings**, as modest yield declines are offset by slowing growth, geopolitical uncertainty, and investor skepticism about AI profit durability.
 The 10-year average forward P/E is approximately 17–18x, making current valuations roughly **3–4 turns rich** on a historical basis. 
Forward P/E is near a level not seen since the internet bubble of 2000.


---

## **1. Regime Call: Late-Cycle**

**This is a late-cycle regime masquerading as mid-cycle.** The combination of re-accelerating inflation, a Fed that just flipped its dot plot from cuts to a potential hike, real rates that are barely restrictive relative to 4.2% CPI, 
front-loaded hawkishness with materially higher near-term inflation alongside slightly softer growth
, a labor market running hot at ~190K/month, and credit spreads still compressed defines the textbook late-cycle configuration — not early-cycle broadening. The bull steepener everyone hoped for never arrived; instead the curve re-normalized from the long end up (bear steepener), which historically compresses multiples and rewards quality over beta.

---

## **2. What's Working / What's Breaking**

**What's working:** 
Semiconductor companies remain the primary direct beneficiaries of the AI capex supercycle, and tech hardware, industrials, and utilities companies are also receiving a large earnings boost from the AI buildout.
 Energy is a standout — 
with oil futures pricing roughly $85/barrel through year-end
, E&P and integrated majors benefit from both elevated realizations and inflation pass-through. 
Only the Energy sector has notable breadth in terms of contributing earnings stocks.
 Financials benefit from a steepening curve and wider NIM. Short-duration, high-quality credit (IG over HY at these spread levels) outperforms as rate volatility stays elevated.

**What's breaking:** Rate-sensitive sectors — residential REITs, homebuilders, utilities with heavy debt loads — are structurally challenged with the 30Y at 4.93%. 
Real wage growth is negative, even as the total payroll level remains elevated
, which is beginning to crimp consumer discretionary. Small-caps (Russell 2000) are a trap: floating-rate debt burdens are rising as any rate-cut catalyst evaporates. 
The AI "circular financing" problem is acute: the very companies that need continued hyperscaler capex to justify their earnings trajectories are the same companies whose results depend on one another's spending.
 Mega-cap tech concentration risk is elevated. Long-duration growth at any price is dead money in a world where the Fed is threatening hikes, not cuts.

---

## **3. What to Watch — Top 2–3 Highest-Impact Events**

1. **June CPI (release ~July 10) & May PCE (July 30):** 
Every incoming CPI print and PCE revision now carries more weight because the Fed has explicitly refused to anchor expectations between meetings.
 A June CPI above 4.0% YoY or a core PCE re-acceleration toward 3.5%+ would likely trigger immediate repricing of a July or September hike, sending 2Y yields through 4.50% and compressing equity multiples sharply. A surprise deceleration is the single biggest positive catalyst.

2. **July 29–30 FOMC Meeting:** 
The committee is more worried about inflation running hot than about the labor market cracking — the configuration that keeps policy restrictive rather than easing.
 With nine dots projecting a hike, 
short-term Treasury yields may rise further over the near term.
 Any language shift back toward cuts is a powerful equity tailwind; any hike execution would be the most significant negative macro shock of the cycle.

3. **Geopolitical / Fiscal Tape — Middle East & US Debt Ceiling/Budget Reconciliation:** 
ISM Manufacturing is already being distorted by businesses rushing to build inventory ahead of supply disruptions from geopolitical conflict
, and 
oil markets are pricing $85/barrel through year-end
 partly on this basis. Any escalation pushes energy costs higher, re-ignites headline CPI, and eliminates any residual dovish optionality for the Fed. Separately, the Congressional budget reconciliation process (tax cuts + deficit expansion) poses an upside supply shock to long-end Treasuries — the 30Y at 4.93% could test 5.25%+ if the fiscal trajectory deteriorates, which would be the most direct threat to the 21x forward multiple holding.

---
*All data sourced from FOMC June 17, 2026 SEP, StreetStats, Nuveen, Goldman Sachs, Schwab, and Federal Reserve/FRED. Data as of June 17–19, 2026.*
